1: pas gar archiereus ex anthrōpōn lambanomenos hyper anthrōpōn kathistatai ta pros ton theon — "for every high priest chosen from among men is appointed to act on behalf of men in relation to God" (ex anthrōpōn lambanomenos: taken from among human beings — the high priest's humanity is a qualification, not a limitation): ta pros ton theon (things toward God — the priestly representative function): the preposition pros indicates directional orientation; the priest stands between human beings and God, facing toward God on their behalf. The two qualifications (v1: chosen from humans; v4: called by God) define the legitimate priesthood.
2: metriopathein dynatos tois agnoousin kai planōmenois, epei kai autos perikeitai astheneian — "he can deal gently with the ignorant and wayward, since he himself is beset with weakness" (metriopathein: to moderate one's feelings, to deal with measured emotion — a Stoic philosophical term for the appropriate emotional response between apathy and passion): the high priest's capacity for gentle response (metriopathein) is grounded in his own weakness-experience. The contrast with the Son's sympathy (4:15) is instructive: the Levitical priest sympathizes because he is himself weak; the Son sympathizes because he has been tested.
7: hos en tais hēmerais tēs sarkos autou deēseis te kai hiketerias pros ton dynamenon sōzein auton ek thanatou meta kraugēs ischyras kai dakryōn prosenenkas kai eisakoustheis apo tēs eulabeias — "in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence" (hiketerias: supplications, olive-branch petitions — the formal term for desperate appeal to a superior; the word is used in Gk papyri for formal petition): the Gethsemane prayer is described without being cited. Eisakoustheis apo tēs eulabeias — "heard because of his reverence" (eulabeia: godly fear, reverent awe — not anxiety but the reverent-careful posture before God): the prayer was heard — not by removal of death but by resurrection through death.
8: kaiper ōn huios, emathen aph' hōn epathen tēn hypakoēn — "although he was a Son, he learned obedience through what he suffered" (emathen aph' hōn epathen: learned from/out of what he suffered — the deliberate Greek wordplay on emathen [learned, aorist of manthanō] and epathen [suffered, aorist of paschō]; the rhyme and etymology emphasize the connection between suffering and learning): the learning is not correcting a moral deficiency but the vocational acquisition of lived obedience — the Son did not know obedience by abstract divine knowledge but through the experiential path of incarnate life. Kaiper ōn huios (although being Son): the divine sonship does not exempt from the learning-through-suffering path; it is the Son who walks this path.
9: kai teleiōtheis egeneto pasin tois hypakouousin autō aitios sōtērias aiōniou — "and being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him" (teleiōtheis: having been made perfect/complete/brought to the goal — aorist passive participle): the perfection (teleiōsis) through suffering and obedience is the high-priestly qualification. Aitios sōtērias aiōniou (cause/source/author of eternal salvation) — the aitios (cause, responsible party) is a philosophical and legal term; the Son is the efficient cause of the salvation that is aiōnios (eternal, age-spanning — not merely long-lasting but of the quality of the coming age).
11: peri hou polys hēmin ho logos kai dysermēneutos legein, epei nōthroi gegonate tais akoais — "about this we have much to say, and it is hard to explain, since you have become dull of hearing" (nōthroi tais akoais: dull in the ears/hearing — nōthros: sluggish, slow; the same word in 6:12 for sluggishness in faith): the pedagogical self-interruption — the author acknowledges difficulty in communicating and attributes it to the community's spiritual torpor, not the topic's intrinsic inaccessibility. The Melchizedek exposition that follows is difficult not because the truth is obscure but because the community lacks the spiritual attentiveness to receive it.
3: kai di' autēn opheilei, kathōs peri tou laou, houtōs kai peri heautou prospherein peri hamartiōn — "This is why he has to offer sacrifices for his own sins as well as for the sins of the people" (opheilei: he is obligated, he owes it — present indicative: the duty is ongoing and inherent to the Levitical priestly role): peri heautou (for himself) — the Levitical high priest's personal sin-offering on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:6, 11) is the defining difference between the Aaronic and Melchizedekian priesthoods. The Son's priesthood, by contrast, does not require self-offering for personal sin (7:27 will make this explicit). The requirement functions here to establish the contrast, not to disparage the Aaronic system.
4: kai ouch heautō tis lambanei tēn timēn, alla kaloumenos hypo tou theou, kathōsper kai Aarōn — "And no one takes this honor on himself, but he receives it when called by God, just as Aaron was" (timēn: honor — G5092; also price/value): priestly appointment is by divine call (kaloumenos hypo tou theou — present passive participle: one being called), not self-designation. The Aaron-precedent is the standard: the Aaronic line did not establish itself but was appointed. The negative (ouch heautō: not for himself) establishes the illegitimacy of self-appointment before the positive example.
5: houtōs kai ho Christos ouch heauton edoxasen genēthēnai archierea, all' ho lalēsas pros auton, Huios mou ei sy, egō sēmeron gegennēka se — "In the same way, Christ did not take on himself the glory of becoming a high priest. But God said to him: You are my Son; today I have become your Father" (Ps 2:7 LXX): edoxasen genēthēnai archierea (glorified/honored to become high priest): the honor of the high priesthood is expressed as divine glorification. The Ps 2:7 citation establishes the Son's divine appointment as the first of two parallel appointments — sonship first, then priesthood (v6). Sēmeron gegennēka se (today I have begotten you): the eschatological "today" of the resurrection-exaltation is the moment when the divine appointment to sonship is publicly declared (cf. Acts 13:33).
6: kathōs kai en heterō legei, Sy hiereus eis ton aiōna kata tēn taxin Melchisedek — "And he says in another place: You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (Ps 110:4 LXX): en heterō (in another place — a second scriptural location); the two citations (Ps 2:7 + Ps 110:4) together constitute the dual divine appointment: sonship and priesthood. Eis ton aiōna (forever, unto the age): the eternal duration of the Melchizedekian priesthood contrasts with the Aaronic, which ended with the priest's death. Kata tēn taxin Melchisedek (according to the order/rank of Melchizedek — taxis: order, arrangement, rank): not the order as a historical institution but a pattern defined by Melchizedek's profile — priest and king without recorded genealogical succession.
10: prosagoreuthteis hypo tou theou archiereus kata tēn taxin Melchisedek — "and was designated by God to be high priest in the order of Melchizedek" (prosagoreuthteis: aorist passive participle of prosagoreuō: to address by name, to designate formally — a NT hapax): the formal designation by name/title is the culmination of the preceding argument. The Melchizedek order is first invoked here, then the author declares he has more to say but must pause due to the community's spiritual immaturity (v11). The Melchizedek exposition is deferred to chapters 7-10, with this verse as the bridge.
12: kai gar opheilontes einai didaskaloi dia ton chronon, palin chreian echete tou didaskein hymas tina ta stoicheia tēs archēs tōn logiōn tou theou, kai gegonate chreian echontes galaktos kai ou stereās trophēs — "In fact, though by this time you ought to be teachers, you need someone to teach you the elementary truths of God's word all over again. You need milk, not solid food" (dia ton chronon: because of/through the time — the time-span of their discipleship should have produced teachers; the failure is of growth, not initial hearing): stoicheia tēs archēs (elementary principles of the beginning — stoicheia: letters of the alphabet, basic elements; archē: beginning): the ABCs of the faith. Logiōn tou theou (the oracles of God — logion: a divine utterance, oracle): the OT scriptures as the authoritative divine speech. Galaktos (milk) vs. stereās trophēs (solid food): the dietary metaphor for theological development is Hellenistic and Jewish.
13: pas gar ho metechōn galaktos apeiros logou dikaiosynēs, nēpios gar estin — "Anyone who lives on milk is still an infant, unskilled in the teaching about righteousness" (apeiros: inexperienced, unskilled — from a-privative + peira: experience; one who has not practiced): logou dikaiosynēs (the word/teaching of righteousness): the genitive is explanatory — the teaching whose content is dikaiosynē. In context, dikaiosynē likely refers to the right-standing and right-conduct that flows from mature Christological understanding, not justification in the Pauline sense. Nēpios gar estin (for he is an infant): nēpios (G3516: infant, one not yet speaking — nē + epos: non-word; one who has no mature speech).
14: teleiōn de estin hē sterea trophē, tōn dia tēn hexin ta aisthētēria gegymnasmena echontōn pros diakrisin kalou te kai kakou — "But solid food is for the mature, who by constant use have trained themselves to distinguish good from evil" (teleiōn: of the mature/complete — G5046; the telos-root: those who have reached their purpose): hexis (habit, constant practice — G1838; the state resulting from repeated exercise): the hexis is not a natural aptitude but an acquired state from repeated practice (dia tēn hexin). Aisthētēria (perceptual organs of moral discernment — G145; a philosophical term for the sensory-like faculties of moral perception): the aisthētēria are trained (gegymnasmena: perfect passive — permanently exercised, as an athlete trains) toward diakrisin kalou te kai kakou (discernment of good and evil). The mature discernment of the Melchizedek material requires exactly this kind of trained moral-theological perception.
